key_insight: Human chemists and an LLM can each propose viable synthesis recipes, and together they can accelerate discovery of new materials—here enabling the discovery of Ba3PtO5 with a new structural prototype.
engineering_relevance: Closed-loop human–AI synthesis planning can shorten the path from candidate generation to experimentally validated materials, which is valuable for scalable materials design pipelines.
method: Generated synthesis recipes for known and unknown Ruddlesden-Popper-related materials using both humans and an LLM.
Tested the recipes in-lab and fed experimental outcomes back into the collaboration loop for a second round.
Focused on the Ruddlesden-Popper homologous series as a well-studied but still discovery-rich phase space.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports similar experimental success rates for humans and LLMs across rounds, with high performance on known materials and much lower but nonzero success on unknown materials, culminating in discovery of Ba3PtO5.
watch_item: Results depend on the chosen materials family and lab validation workflow, so performance may not generalize to other chemistries or synthesis constraints.
paper_title: Human and LLM Collaboration for Accelerated Materials Synthesis and Discovery
